text: What does research on behaviorism teach us about human learning?

options.text: Repeatedly pairing a neutral stimulus with a reward can lead to a positive response to the neutral stimulus ("conditioning") over time
Between stimulus and response, there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response.
Stimuli only lead to responses after a careful elaborated system II processing.
Sanctions and rewards are not enough to train complex behaviours through reinforcement.

text: Which human cognitive process have recent LLM models started to mimick (since GPT 3/ DeepSeek)?

options.text: Classical conditioning.
System II thinking ("elaboration").
Intelligence G-factor.
Working memory ("extraction").

text: What are the stages of human memory processing?

options.text: Enconding, excitation, attribution.
Decyphering, storage, attribution.
Appraisal, excitation, retrieval.
Encoding, storage, retrieval.

text: Which of the following statements best reflects the current state of evidence around AI and learning?

options.text: People that google information can distinguish between "knowing something" and "having looked up" something.
AI-enhanced learning always leads to worse learning outcomes.
Getting a direct answer from an AI-chatbot for your question is the optimal way of ensuring durable learning experiences.
People who Googled information perceive themselves as smarter than those who did not look up the content.

text: What does the neurological "blind spot" tell us about human perception?

options.text: Every person brings individual biases.
Visual perception is partially constructed in the human brain.
Certain neurological areas cannot be analysed in medical examinations.
Computer vision is generally superior to human vision.

text: How often do humans engage in social cognition (thinking about themselves or other people)?

options.text: Most of the time. Social cognition is likely the "default" or "baseline" setting of our brain
Sometimes we communicative with someone.
Rarely, only in the context of direct interactions with others.
Only when we see/hear or otherwise perceive someone.

text: Which of the following is part of our self-concept?

options.text: A constantly changing self concept that does not include stable concepts.
An independent self concept that is unaffected by our environment.
A primary self concept that is formed in our childhood and then never changes.
A working self concept that varies across situations.

text: Which biases can affect primarily LLMs?

options.text: Fundamental attribution errors
Pattern and averaging biases
Illusory correlations
Status quo biases

text: Communication is…

options.text: Inevitable: one cannot *not* communicate
Universal: interpretations are the same across contexts.
Clear-cut: the start of a communication episode is easy to determine.
Emotional: emotions arise from all signs and symbols.

text: What does the "Limited Capacity Model of Motivated Mediated Message Processing" (LC4M) postulate?

options.text: Consuming media requires cognitive ressources for encoding, storage, and retrieval.
Consuming media requires only cognitive ressources for the retrieval of information.
Consuming media requires only cognitive ressources for the selection of information to encode.
Consuming media does not require cognitive ressources.

text: What is a social innovation?

options.text: New forms of digital social interactions.
A new market for a social network.
A new organisational structure.
Something that contributes to the advancement of society (e.g., in terms of overall wellbeing).

text: What determines if an innovation diffuses through a social network?

options.text: Whether it is a new method.
Wether it is max distinct from everything that came before.
How (positively or negatively) people perceive the innovation.
Whether it breaks the most existing regulations.

text: Which of the following is not a problem/ risk when using current AI models for journalistic fact-checking?

options.text: Loss of trust among the audience.
Identifying claims.
Source attribution errors.
Copyright issues.

text: What does the distinction between avatars and agents mean for autonomous warfare?

options.text: The amount of human targeting: agents act autonomously, avatars are controlled. Thus the UN rejects agent-based weapon systems that target specific humans.
The amount of human communication ability: agents are more capable of human communication than avatars. Thus, the UN recommends only agent-based weapon systems.
The amount of bias: agents are less biased than avatars. Thus the UN, recommends only agent-based weapon systems.
The amount of human control: agents act autonomously, avatars are controlled. Thus, the UN rejects agent-based weapon systems.

text: What does the media equation predict for humans' response to AI-chatbots such as Claude?

options.text: Chatbots will  shift the vast majority of media jobs to AI-enhanced jobs.
They will treat them like other social actors.
They will treat them like other news sources.
Audiences will replace other media sources through such chatbots.

text: What is the "uncanny valley" effect?

options.text: An ethical dilema in autonomous warfare.
An impactful study with human-like robots was conducted in Silicon Valley.
The economic success of tech companies based in Silicon Valley vs. other places.
A description of the non-linear relationship between the human-likeness of artificial others and how much humans like these artificial others.

text: What is a current frontier when it comes to improving recommender systems?

options.text: Multi-modal recommendation.
Integrating deep-learning.
Collaborative filtering algorithms.
Text-based recommendation.

text: How can algorithms "for societal good" look like?

options.text: They recommed content that is more attention-grabbing.
They promote collective well-being and align with human-rights.
They prevent the exposure to content that you disagree with.
They decrease the need for autonomously picking content.

text: What do psychologists describe as "motivated cognition"?

options.text: The cognitive process of elaboration is needed to motivate behavior.
When we are motivated, our cognitive processes works faster, such that our overall intelligence varies across situations.
We need to be highly motivated to engage in cognitive processes so that System I thinking occurs only when we are highly motivated.
The motivation to be accurate or confirm prior beliefs influences how we process information, such that we prefer information that confirms our prior beliefs.

text: What is "micro-targeting"?

options.text: Aggregating small micro-groups to larger clusters in digital content analysis.
Neural-link devices that use EEG patterns to enable  hand-free micro-level device control.
Algorithms that target micro-dynamics to shape the toxicity of online discourses.
Forming small (micro) groups based on digital data and sending them personalized messages.

text: What does the "Spiral of Silence" theory postulate?

options.text: The effect of moderation removing hateful comments on the reduced participation of other users.
People's tendency to be more likely to voice their opinion when they think that they hold a majority opinion, and to stay silent when they feel they are in the minority.
LLMs' tendency to misrepresent minority dialects, with models building on previous models showing an ever larger "silencing" effect.
The observation that once digital discourses have amassed enough hateful comments, most participants become silent

text: Which of the following plays a central role in the formation of intimate relationships between humans and chatbots?

options.text: Depressions.
Negative social relations with parents.
Self and partner disclosure.
Insecurity.

text: Which of the following are the classic components of the Internet of Things (IOT)?

options.text: Sensors, automation, algorithms, and deep learning.
Automation and algorithms.
Sensors, automation, and algorithms.
Sensors and automation.

text: What would the "Social Media Privacy Model" predict for self-disclosure when smart wearables such as Meta's RayBan glasses are becomming for common in private spaces?

options.text: People give up and just share everything without caring.
People don't interact anymore with people wearing smart glasses.
People increasingly trust social media companies to handle their data well.
People adapt their level of self-disclosure.

text: What defines social media?

options.text: Digitally networked technologies that enable information sharing and real-time interaction with others.
Digitally networked technologies that enable information sharing and interacting with others.
Digitally networked technologies.
Digitally networked technologies that enable information sharing.

text: Which of the following describes the relationship between social media use and posting behavior in Denmark?

options.text: There are more than ten times as many Danes who use social media as there are Danes who share political opinions on social media.
There are more than four times as many Danes who use social media as there are Danes who share political opinions on social media.
There are as many Danes who use social media as there are Danes who share political opinions on social media.
There are more than twice as many Danes who use social media as there are Danes who share political opinions on social media.

text: What are typical barriers to AI adaption in public administration?

options.text: Lack of relevant products targeted towards public administration.
Lack of tasks within public administration that could be automated.
Leadership mismanagement.
Organizational culture (not change-oriented) and environmental context (e.g., political pressure).

context: Which of the following statements about misinformation is itself a misinformation (aka false).
text: Which of the following statements about misinformation is itself a misinformation (aka false)?

options.text: More than half of the danish Tweets around COVID-19 masks were misinformation.
More than half of Danish children report prior exposure to misinformation.
More than half of Danes are concerned about misinformation.
More than half of the misinformation about different events typically comes from fewer than 10% of the accounts, i.e., so-called superspreaders.

text: How often do you need to test the performance/ risk of a mathematical model using generative AI in the finance sector?

options.text: During development, before application, and continuous monitoring.
During development.
During development and before application.
Continous monitoring.

text: Which of the following  job-types is currently most affected by AI?

options.text: Software developers (entry level).
Risk and business analysts.
Digital communication professionals.
Coordinators.

text: Which of the following statements best reflects the current similarity of AI responses to human delusion versus clinical principles in working with delusional patients?

options.text: Young adults with a high risk for delusions are less likely to use AI chatbots than their peers without such a risk profile.
AI chatbots implement clinical principles for working with delusions.
There are several aspects where LLMs do violate clinical principles for responding to delusion.
AI chatbots endorse delusions and violence in most of the conversations with delusional patients.

text: Based on the currently available evidence: which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: AI is worse than humans in providing psychotherapy.
AI is worse than humans in judging mamography images.
AI is worse than humans in making triage decisions in an emergency room.
AI is worse than humans in detecting depression from physiological data.

text: How does the "Uses & Gratifications" approach conceptualize media use?

options.text: As two processes: hedonic (fun, pleasure) and eudaimonic (aka "meaningful") entertainment sought through media use.
As active or passive media use patterns.
As a process that includes the gratifications users hope for, their assessment of whether a medium can give them these gratifications, and an evaluation of the gratifications actually obtained.
As a cognitive process in which media use momentarily expands the boundaries of the self.

text: Imagine your neighbor is concerned because her son is constantly in front of his PlayStation: When do you think her concerns are justified?

options.text: When he plays for wining competitions.
When he plays to escape his offline problems.
When he plays to get entertained.
When he plays to meet with friends.

text: Based on the current state of evidence: Which of the following statements is false?

options.text: Playing a queer avatar in VR decreases your in-game performance in shooters.
Playing a homeless avatar in VR motivates signing a petition agains homelessness.
Playing a Jamacanian avatar in VR can increase your drumming skills.
Playing a Black avatar in VR reduces sterotypes among White Americans.
